edges.rationale: Realism is a position within metaethics about the status of moral facts.
Anti-realism is the metaethical denial of stance-independent moral facts.
The definitional opposition over whether moral facts exist mind-independently.
Cognitivism is a metaethical thesis about moral judgment and truth-aptness.
Whether moral judgments express truth-apt beliefs or non-cognitive attitudes.
Non-cognitivism is the classic expression-based form of anti-realism.
Emotivism treats moral utterances as expressions of feeling, not beliefs.
Prescriptivism treats moral judgments as universal prescriptions, not descriptions.
Expressivism is the contemporary refinement of non-cognitivism.
Language, Truth and Logic ch. 6 gives the classic emotivist account.
Stevenson's Ethics and Language (1944) is the developed emotivism.
Hare's universal prescriptivism (The Language of Morals, 1952).
Blackburn's projectivist expressivism is one of its two canonical statements.
Gibbard's norm-expressivism (Wise Choices, Apt Feelings) extends the program.
Quasi-realism is Blackburn's own coinage and program.
Quasi-realism builds on an expressivist base to earn realist-sounding talk.
It aims to recover realist discourse without realist metaphysics.
Mackie: moral judgments are beliefs, and all systematically false.
Chapter 1 ('The Subjectivity of Values') states the error theory.
Error theory is the cognitivist form of anti-realism.
Nihilism denies that anything is morally right or wrong.
Subjectivism makes moral truth depend on attitudes of subjects.
Metaethical relativism relativises moral truth to cultures or frameworks.
Naturalist realism: moral facts are natural facts.
Non-naturalist realism: moral facts are sui generis, not natural.
The intra-realist dispute over the nature of moral properties.
Principia Ethica: good is a simple, non-natural, indefinable property.
Classical intuitionism (Moore, Prichard, Ross) is non-naturalist realism.
Prichard's 1912 paper is the arch-statement of Oxford intuitionism.
Ross systematised intuitionism with the prima facie duty framework.
Moore's charge that defining good in natural terms is fallacious.
The fallacy charge is aimed at naturalist definitions of good.
Moore's argument that any naturalist definition leaves an open question.
Its target is the identification of goodness with any natural property.
Ross's framework of conditional duties in The Right and the Good (1930).
Hutcheson's moral sense theory founded 18th-century sentimentalism.
Hume grounded moral distinctions in sentiment, not reason.
The 18th-century debate: moral sense vs reason as the source of morals.
Hume's observation that ought cannot be derived from is ('Hume's Law').
Particularist holism developed through critical engagement with Ross's framework.

proposed_nodes.label: Jonathan Dancy
proposed_nodes.reason: The principal developer of moral particularism (Ethics Without Principles); needed for its DEVELOPED_BY edge.

proposed_nodes.label: The Language of Morals
proposed_nodes.reason: Hare 1952; the work that introduced universal prescriptivism.
